_comment: Relevance judgments for the sample corpus, used by section_vs_chunk_retrieval.py.
Write the same shape for your own corpus and pass it with --judgments.
query             : the search string, exactly as a user would type it.
relevant_docs     : document ids that answer it. upsert_from_file() ids a
document by its filename stem, so 'data-retention.md'
relevant_sections : '<doc id>::<section heading>', matched against the
section_heading metadata on each result. Headings are
used rather than section indices because you can read a
heading off the document; an index you would have to
These are binary judgments (relevant / not). Grading them 0-2 would be more
informative and much more work; binary is what most people will actually do.

queries.query: when should I roll back a deploy instead of investigating the cause first?
how long is customer data kept after an account is closed?
who is in charge during an incident and what are they responsible for?
how do I change a database schema safely?
what actually happens when a customer asks for their data to be deleted?
how much real traffic does a change see before it is fully released?
what counts as an incident worth declaring?
who can read customer content in production and is it logged?
how do we decide how serious an incident is?
when is an incident considered finished?
how is expired data actually destroyed and is it checked?
